title: Oslo

jokes.text: Selina Meyer! The woman who freed Tibet!
jokes.setup: The previously recapped events established Selina's Tibet diplomatic achievement as her signature legacy moment
jokes.punchline: The phrase 'woman who freed Tibet' is deployed with such breathless enthusiasm that it immediately signals this will be her hollow defining credential throughout the episode
jokes.explanation: Works as an establishing joke for the episode's central irony — Selina's greatest achievement is about to be sold off for political favors. The enthusiasm of the greeting underscores her narcissistic pleasure in being celebrated for something she'll soon betray.

jokes.text: It's kinda like the Peace Prize Junior.
jokes.setup: Selina is being congratulated on winning what everyone is calling her Nobel Peace Prize
jokes.punchline: An aide clarifies it's actually the 'Summit Peace Award of the World Summit of Nobel Peace Laureates' — i.e., a knock-off
jokes.explanation: The layered diminishment — first the full absurd title, then 'Peace Prize Junior' — is economical and sharp. It perfectly captures Veep's thesis that Selina's achievements are always slightly less than they appear.

jokes.text: The American voters don't know the difference, and, frankly, neither do I.
jokes.setup: An aide has just clarified the prize is NOT a real Nobel Peace Prize
jokes.punchline: Selina dismisses the distinction by lumping herself in with the ignorant electorate she's about to manipulate
jokes.explanation: Perfectly captures Selina's blend of cynicism and obliviousness. The self-inclusion ('neither do I') is the key move — it could read as self-deprecating, but it's actually her genuine indifference to truth. Only Selina could say this line. Strong economy, sharp character work.

jokes.text: I wanna sound like Bono trying to impress his own reflection in the mirror.
jokes.setup: Selina is instructing Leon to write a grandiose, self-aggrandizing acceptance speech
jokes.punchline: The Bono simile perfectly skewers both celebrity humanitarianism and Selina's own performative vanity
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best lines. The image of Bono impressing his own reflection layers celebrity narcissism, self-righteousness, and vanity in a single vivid phrase. The 'mirror' element is the key escalation — it's not just impressing an audience, it's self-worship. Highly quotable, strong originality.

jokes.text: Tibet, Tibet, Tibet. / You bet, you bet, you bet.
jokes.setup: Selina instructs Leon to emphasize Tibet in the speech
jokes.punchline: Leon's affirmative 'you bet, you bet, you bet' inadvertently rhymes with 'Tibet' creating a rapid-fire comic rhythm
jokes.explanation: A quick, light wordplay beat. The rhyme is probably intentional on the writers' part, creating a satisfying rhythmic close to the exchange. Not deep, but punchy and well-timed.

jokes.text: Hey-- Was that Colonel Al-Saleh, the Butcher of Juba, back there by the carving station?
jokes.setup: The Nobel Peace Prize ceremony, which should be a gathering of moral exemplars
jokes.punchline: Selina casually identifies a war criminal near the buffet, as if spotting a celebrity at a party
jokes.explanation: The juxtaposition of 'Butcher of Juba' and 'carving station' is a masterstroke — the catering detail domesticates the atrocity while also making a dark pun. Sharp dark comedy with strong economy. Also functions as setup for the follow-up joke.

jokes.text: The Nobel people don't ask too many questions if you buy a table. Ma'am, at this point, they're just trying to avoid losing the building.
jokes.setup: Selina noticed a war criminal at the Nobel ceremony
jokes.punchline: The aide explains that the Nobel organization has been reduced to a financially desperate institution that lets anyone in with a checkbook
jokes.explanation: Escalates the corruption of the Nobel brand effectively. The specificity of 'avoid losing the building' elevates it from a generic cynicism joke to something more precise and funnier.

jokes.text: My Nobel prize really makes the point that I've got way more foreign policy experience than that half-wit Kemi.
jokes.setup: Selina has just been told it's not even a real Nobel Prize
jokes.punchline: She immediately weaponizes her fake prize against a political rival, demonstrating total imperviousness to the facts she just heard
jokes.explanation: Functions as a callback to the 'Peace Prize Junior' joke — Selina has already pivoted to using the fake award as genuine currency. Her obliviousness is the joke, and it's in character.

jokes.text: I think it's pronounced 'half-white.' / Well, 'tomato, mulatto.'
jokes.setup: Selina called Kemi a 'half-wit'
jokes.punchline: A staffer mishears it as a racial slur, and Selina's correction — 'tomato, mulatto' — is a racist malapropism that reveals her casual bigotry
jokes.explanation: Exceptional construction. The mishearing creates ambiguity (is Selina racist?), then her 'correction' confirms she is while also misusing 'mulatto' as a rhyme for 'tomato.' The phrase 'tomato, mulatto' is brilliantly structured as a riff on 'tomato, tomato' — casual, dismissive, and deeply revealing about Selina's character. Very quotable, strong rewatch value.

jokes.text: as long as someone lets me have mini-vegan pigs in gluten-free blankets at the reception
jokes.setup: Selina is telling Catherine and Marjorie she's fine with whatever they want for their wedding
jokes.punchline: Her 'whatever you want' is immediately undercut by a very specific food demand that combines contradictory dietary trends absurdly
jokes.explanation: The absurdity of 'vegan pigs in gluten-free blankets' works as a comment on high-end food trends and Selina's self-centered version of 'being supportive.' Competent character comedy.

jokes.text: Because without an American marriage license, U.S. community property laws do not apply, my darling.
jokes.setup: Selina suggests Catherine get married in Europe, framed as romantic
jokes.punchline: Selina immediately reveals she's suggesting it to protect her own assets from Marjorie in a divorce
jokes.explanation: A perfect Selina moment — her apparent maternal concern is instantly revealed as financial scheming against her own daughter's marriage. The legal specificity ('community property laws') makes it feel both researched and more callous. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: That's fine. You and your maid of honor here can sit me next to whatever Injun Joe you want.
jokes.setup: Selina is supposedly being gracious about the wedding seating
jokes.punchline: She refers to Marjorie's shaman as 'Injun Joe' — a casual racist dismissal — while framing it as magnanimity
jokes.explanation: Another casual Selina bigotry joke; works but is slightly cheaper than 'tomato, mulatto' since it's a more direct slur without the structural elegance of the earlier joke.

jokes.text: Ma'am, Lu's people have been Great-Walling us.
jokes.setup: The team is trying to get a meeting with Chinese President Lu
jokes.punchline: The aide coins 'Great-Walling' as a verb meaning stonewalling, a China-specific spin on the cliché
jokes.explanation: A clever neologism that earns its laugh through context-appropriate wordplay. Iannucci's writers love these coined diplomatic verbs and this one lands well.

jokes.text: Lu developed a real hankering for Finnish furburger back at Camp David. He eye-fucked her so bad he got retinal herpes.
jokes.setup: Selina explains she's using Minna as bait to get a meeting with Chinese President Lu
jokes.punchline: The escalating vulgarity — 'Finnish furburger' to 'retinal herpes' — describes Lu's attraction to Minna in maximally crude terms
jokes.explanation: 'Retinal herpes' is a genuinely inventive coinage — the escalation from visual attraction to a STI contracted through eye contact is both absurd and perfectly structured. 'Finnish furburger' as a euphemism is crude but specific. The line earns its vulgarity through escalation.

jokes.text: I don't get the attraction. / Oh, I do.
jokes.setup: After the 'retinal herpes' line, two staffers watch Minna and Selina embrace
jokes.punchline: The immediate disagreement between two background characters — one confused by Lu's attraction to Minna, the other very much not — lands as a quick visual reaction beat
jokes.explanation: A quick two-line reaction beat that works through economy and timing. Not deep, but a competent tag on the previous joke.

jokes.text: So, my last three lovers, they are complaining that my naughty talk is both incessant and soporific. / Maybe you should let 'em choke you. / You think they would rather choke me than listen to me talk? / I can only speak for myself.
jokes.setup: Minna complains her pillow talk is putting partners to sleep
jokes.punchline: Selina suggests choking, then when Minna asks for clarification, Selina admits she personally would prefer to choke Minna rather than listen to her
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best exchanges. The setup is a Minna overshare about her sex life; the misdirection is Selina appearing to give kink advice; the punchline ('I can only speak for myself') reveals Selina would personally choose to choke Minna over listen to her — a devastating expression of their frenemy dynamic. The structure is near-perfect: three beats, each escalating, landing on a beautifully ambiguous final line.

jokes.text: Oh, my gosh, I didn't know you were still alive. / The Russians reinstalled me when they invaded Georgia. Ha! / Factory reboot. / Both: Beep-boop-beep-beep-boop.
jokes.setup: Selina is surprised to see Murman, the Georgian oligarch/politician
jokes.punchline: Murman explains his political resurrection via Russian invasion ('reinstalled') and then both characters perform a robot 'reboot' sound together, humanizing an otherwise dark geopolitical joke
jokes.explanation: The 'factory reboot' metaphor for political reinstatement under Russian occupation is dark and sharp, and the shared 'beep-boop' between Selina and Murman is a charming absurdist payoff that shows genuine comedic chemistry. The escalation from geopolitical dark joke to silly sound effects is delightful.

jokes.text: Both: Beep-boop-beep-beep-boop.
jokes.setup: Murman explains he was 'factory rebooted' by the Russians
jokes.punchline: Both Selina and Murman perform the factory reboot sound together as a shared joke, normalizing the absurdity
jokes.explanation: Two world leaders making robot sounds together is a pitch-perfect absurdist visual/audio gag. The shared performance implies this is a recurring bit between them, which makes it stranger and funnier.

jokes.text: I recently bought an English football club. / Oh, wonderful! / Leeds United. / No English players, so pretty good team.
jokes.setup: Murman announces he bought an English football club
jokes.punchline: Leeds United, then the logic that having no English players makes it a good team
jokes.explanation: A tidy football/oligarch joke with the Leeds specificity doing good work. The anti-English players logic is a dig at both English football quality and foreign club ownership. Works better if you know English football.

jokes.text: What if I gave you money to help you win your election? / America does not stand for foreign interference in our elections! Who do you think that I am? / Yes, of course. Of course. / It's an outrage! / New topic. New topic. / I want to buy your Palm Beach house for $114 million.
jokes.setup: Murman offers Selina money for her election campaign
jokes.punchline: Selina performs righteous indignation about foreign interference — then immediately pivots to accepting the same thing framed as a real estate deal
jokes.explanation: Structurally excellent. Selina's theatrical outrage at foreign election interference is immediately demolished by her accepting a nearly identical transaction via real estate — a brilliant satirical compression of American political hypocrisy. The speed of the reversal ('New topic. New topic.') is perfect. One of the episode's strongest satirical jokes.

jokes.text: I'd rather deal with the Russian mob than with those real estate people. It's because they're unethical, that's what they are. Because they are working on commissions. Yes! Six percent? It's abject greed, in my view.
jokes.setup: Murman is selling Selina's house through a money-laundering scheme
jokes.punchline: Both Selina and Murman bond over their moral outrage at real estate commissions — from a Russian oligarch and a corrupt ex-president, the most absurdly misplaced indignation possible
jokes.explanation: The extended moral indignation about 6% real estate commissions from two people engaged in literal money laundering is exquisitely calibrated satire. Murman's specificity about the commission rate and 'abject greed' is perfectly wrongheaded. Strong character integration — this conversation only works between these two specific characters.

jokes.text: The 114 million would be wired from the Seychelles to Malta, then back to the Seychelles, and then to you.
jokes.setup: Murman is making what sounds like a legitimate real estate offer
jokes.punchline: He matter-of-factly describes an obvious money-laundering route with complete normality
jokes.explanation: The specificity of the laundering route (Seychelles → Malta → Seychelles → you) delivered with utter casualness is funny. The round-trip through the Seychelles is an especially nice absurdist touch.

jokes.text: White people problems, right?
jokes.setup: After philosophizing about human suffering while discussing money laundering and foreign election interference
jokes.punchline: Murman tags his own existential musing with 'white people problems' — a wildly inappropriate application of the social media phrase to his own corrupt scheming
jokes.explanation: Funny specifically because Murman is using an American colloquialism completely out of context to describe his oligarch problems. His adoption of casual American slang is a character quirk that pays off here.

jokes.text: Lu's looking for the tickee in someone's shirtee.
jokes.setup: Kent is reporting that President Lu is interested in meeting with someone at the Nobel gathering
jokes.punchline: Kent uses a racist caricature of a Chinese accent to convey the information — doubly uncomfortable as a character choice for the prim statistician Kent
jokes.explanation: The joke is slightly uncomfortable — it's a racial accent impression — but the incongruity of Kent doing it is what saves it. Still, it's a relatively cheap move compared to the episode's better jokes.

jokes.text: He seems quite excited. / Yeah, we could see that from here.
jokes.setup: Minna reports that Lu is 'quite excited' to meet with Selina
jokes.punchline: The staffer's response implies they can see Lu's visible physical arousal from across the room — a visual gag played entirely in dialogue
jokes.explanation: A clean double entendre with good setup/punchline structure. Works through implication — the visual gag exists only in the audience's imagination, which is funnier.

jokes.text: Or else we'll tell the whole world how you vaporized a DRA leader's wedding with Hellfire missiles and also took a huge laser-guided shit all over Pakistani sovereignty
jokes.setup: Selina is trying to pressure Keith into getting her a meeting with Lu
jokes.punchline: 'Took a huge laser-guided shit all over Pakistani sovereignty' is a vulgar-military metaphor mash-up that describes a genuine war crime in the most inelegant terms possible
jokes.explanation: 'Laser-guided shit' is an inspired compound — the military precision of the adjective against the vulgarity of the noun creates a delightful dissonance. Veep's signature mode: making foreign policy atrocities funny through language.

jokes.text: It was classified-- until somebody on your staff clicked on 'Asian Girls Bound and Gagged.'
jokes.setup: Keith claims the drone footage is highly classified
jokes.punchline: A staffer on Selina's team was apparently watching fetish porn and inadvertently accessed classified footage — the implication being the classified material and the porn were on the same server or similar path
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly economical — 'Asian Girls Bound and Gagged' explains the entire breach in one phrase while being maximally embarrassing. The specificity of the porn title is the joke. The follow-up ('Oh, Ben. Or Kent. Or Leon. Oh, God. Or Marjorie.') extends it perfectly.

jokes.text: Oh, Ben. / Or Kent. / Or Leon. Oh, God. / Or Marjorie. I mean, I--
jokes.setup: Selina hears that someone on her staff accessed classified material via porn
jokes.punchline: She cycles through her staff members as suspects — each name is funnier, landing on Marjorie (her daughter's fiancée) as the most alarming possibility
jokes.explanation: The escalating list structure works beautifully — each name has its own comedic logic (Ben is gross, Kent is surprising, Leon is mundane, Marjorie is the most horrifying). The trailing 'I mean, I--' as Selina can't even finish the thought is a perfect tag.

jokes.text: Jonah's anti-vaccination message is bringing together an unheard-of mix of Orthodox Jews, uneducated fringe conspiracists, and Kombucha-douching private school moms.
jokes.setup: Campaign update on Jonah's anti-vax coalition
jokes.punchline: 'Kombucha-douching private school moms' as the third coalition member is the punchline — it's the perfect contemporary descriptor for a specific type of affluent anti-vaxxer
jokes.explanation: 'Kombucha-douching' is a remarkable compound adjective — it combines two wellness trends into a single devastating characterization. The list structure (Orthodox Jews / fringe conspiracists / Kombucha-douching moms) builds to the most specific and most contemporary item.

jokes.text: Look. If we go to Kentucky, it's gonna look like a wang. The Great Lakes'll be like the spooey.
jokes.setup: The team is looking at a campaign map
jokes.punchline: Dan describes the Kentucky campaign route as resembling a penis, with the Great Lakes as ejaculate — a vulgar geographic observation delivered as strategic advice
jokes.explanation: Juvenile but effective — the visual logic of the map joke works even without seeing it. 'Spooey' is an odd word choice that adds a layer of childishness appropriate to the Jonah campaign.

jokes.text: Jonah, it's your dad again. It's the tenth time today. / Hey, Siri, you fuck my dad? / Siri: I don't know how to respond to that. / Typical woman.
jokes.setup: Jonah's phone rings with a call from his newly discovered biological father
jokes.punchline: Jonah addresses his phone's AI assistant as if she's a sexual suspect, then dismisses Siri's confusion as typical female evasiveness
jokes.explanation: The Siri joke is perfectly constructed: Jonah's question is non-sequitur enough to confuse the AI, Siri's response is accurate and perfect, and Jonah's interpretation reveals his worldview in one line ('Typical woman'). Only Jonah could make this joke work — it requires his specific brand of aggressive stupidity.

jokes.text: Pennsylvania, you have the second-lowest vaccination rate in the nation, and when I am elected president, you will be number one!
jokes.setup: Jonah is giving a campaign speech
jokes.punchline: He frames being the worst state for anti-vaccination as an achievement to strive toward — a crowd-pleasing call to be maximally dangerous
jokes.explanation: Exceptional political satire. The speech format (crowd cheer, rally energy) applied to the goal of being the worst-vaccinated state is a precise skewering of populist political rhetoric. The structure mimics a genuine campaign promise perfectly — 'you will be number one!' — which makes the inversion funnier. Prescient and sharp.

jokes.text: The menu has been an atrocity. The guests are vegan, and the president won't eat anything without a face.
jokes.setup: Gary is complaining about catering logistics for the Nobel event
jokes.punchline: The use of 'atrocity' for menu problems (foreshadowing the actual war crimes discussion) combined with the opposing dietary requirements creates a miniature crisis
jokes.explanation: 'Won't eat anything without a face' is a funny inversion of vegan logic — Selina requires her food to have been alive. The word 'atrocity' for a menu problem is a nice tonal note that gains resonance when the actual atrocity discussion follows.

jokes.text: So I droned a couple wedding civilians on the wrong side of the Af-Pak border. / I thought you were talking about your daughter's wedding to your homosexual doppelganger.
jokes.setup: Minna says President Lu mentioned a 'complete atrocity'; Selina assumes he told Minna about the drone strike
jokes.punchline: Minna was talking about Catherine's wedding — revealing (a) Selina casually confessed to war crimes, and (b) Minna's description of Catherine as Selina's 'homosexual doppelganger' is its own joke
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. The misdirection is perfectly set up: Selina's paranoid assumption leads her to confess war crimes to Minna when Minna was discussing something entirely different. The structure is flawless — Selina incriminates herself through her own anxiety. 'Homosexual doppelganger' is also a great bonus joke-within-a-joke. This is the kind of comedy that gets better on rewatch because you appreciate the trap being laid.

jokes.text: 'Droned a wedding' is, uh, American slang for... 'I got it, I nailed it.' / No, no, no. Really. Like, 'I droned that wedding, man!' / I do not think that it is slang of any kind. / Oh, I guarantee you it is. Ask anyone... here--
jokes.setup: Selina has accidentally admitted to a war crime and is trying to walk it back
jokes.punchline: Selina invents American slang on the spot and insists it's real — her desperation increasing as Minna refuses to believe her
jokes.explanation: The cringe escalation of Selina frantically inventing slang ('I droned that wedding, man!') is both hilarious and deeply pathetic. Her confidence in the lie combined with Minna's unimpressed disbelief creates mounting tension. 'Ask anyone... here--' cutting off as she realizes she has no witnesses is a perfect structural beat. Only Selina's specific brand of shameless improvisation makes this work.

jokes.text: Mike, we have made a lot of mistakes here at CBS News. / I know, right? / But I think you could be a big part of turning that around.
jokes.setup: Mike is being offered a job as a CBS News correspondent
jokes.punchline: The executive's opening confession ('we've made a lot of mistakes') — presumably meant to be modest — is instead read by Mike as validation of his own opinion of the network, then the executive suggests Mike is the solution, which is itself the joke given Mike's incompetence
jokes.explanation: Works as a character comedy beat for Mike — his enthusiastic 'I know, right?' agreeing with the network's self-flagellation is perfectly oblivious. The joke that Mike is the solution to CBS's problems lands well given his established incompetence.

jokes.text: And the horrible wife? / She's horrible! / A lot of that stuff I didn't know I was doing until people pointed it out. / See? Keep that sense of humor.
jokes.setup: The executive is praising Mike's old show bits, including the 'horrible wife' character
jokes.punchline: Mike genuinely didn't know his wife material was a bit — he was just airing real grievances — and the executive mistakes this for self-awareness
jokes.explanation: A well-constructed bit about Mike's accidental comedy — his marital complaints were apparently unintentional humor. The executive's misreading ('keep that sense of humor') is a nice ironic button.

jokes.text: All the time. Aung San Suu Kyi, Henry Kissinger, and also, the prize that you're getting, it is not the Peace Prize. / Oh, don't start with that! / It is a second-tier Peace Prize. / No. / It is made of very hard plastic.
jokes.setup: Selina asks if they give Nobel Peace Prizes to war criminals
jokes.punchline: Minna says 'all the time,' lists Kissinger and Suu Kyi, then pivots back to the 'not a real Peace Prize' running gag, ending with the devastating physical detail: 'It is made of very hard plastic.'
jokes.explanation: The triple escalation is brilliant: (1) war criminal Peace Prize winners are common, (2) here are two examples that are pointed but accurate, (3) pivot to the running gag about her fake prize, (4) the physical detail 'very hard plastic' as the ultimate humiliation. The Kissinger reference lands as a real-world satirical punch, and 'made of very hard plastic' is the funniest possible specification for a second-tier award.

jokes.text: Ha! / That was your phone.
jokes.setup: Selina makes a threatening gesture toward Minna
jokes.punchline: What Selina thought was a dramatic moment was actually her phone ringing — deflating the menace entirely
jokes.explanation: A quick physical comedy beat — Selina's threatening moment is immediately undercut by her own phone. Simple but effective deflation of the scene's tension.

jokes.text: I called Interpol before we got in the car.
jokes.setup: Selina says Minna 'left her no choice,' implying she'll retaliate
jokes.punchline: Minna calmly reveals she'd already called Interpol before the conversation even began, completely inverting the power dynamic
jokes.explanation: Perfect deadpan economy — 'I called Interpol before we got in the car' is devastating in its calm. Minna's principled placidity against Selina's panicked scheming is a great character contrast.

jokes.text: As the former president of the United States, truth and justice can gargle my balls.
jokes.setup: Minna invokes her commitment to truth and justice as a reason she can't help Selina
jokes.punchline: Selina's response is maximally vulgar and maximally anti-democratic — delivered with presidential authority
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's most memorable lines. The juxtaposition of 'As the former president of the United States' (gravitas, institutional authority) with 'truth and justice can gargle my balls' (utter contempt for the same) is perfectly constructed. Only Selina Meyer could say this line with this framing. Near-perfect character integration. Extremely quotable — the preamble makes it.

jokes.text: That's because Autistic Barbie here tattled on me over the wedding drone strike.
jokes.setup: Selina is told she's wanted by the ICC for war crimes
jokes.punchline: She dismisses Minna as 'Autistic Barbie' and describes a drone strike on a wedding as 'tattling'
jokes.explanation: 'Autistic Barbie' is a mean but vivid insult that captures both Minna's precise rule-following and her physical appearance. The framing of war crimes as 'tattling' is excellent Selina — she's genuinely offended by the social transgression, not the crime. 'Earned_vs_cheap' slightly lower due to the ableism in 'Autistic Barbie.'

jokes.text: I'd just as soon stay at an Embassy Suites.
jokes.setup: Minna offers Selina asylum at the Finnish Embassy rather than face extradition to the Hague
jokes.punchline: Selina's preference for a mid-tier hotel chain over the Embassy is both a class joke and a character joke about her standards
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'Embassy Suites' — a perfectly mid-grade hotel chain — is doing a lot of work. Selina's horror at staying in an actual embassy (presumably less luxurious) is a neat inversion of the privilege she's being offered.

jokes.text: She prefers the word 'spa.'
jokes.setup: Minna has just formally granted Selina asylum
jokes.punchline: Gary immediately reframes 'asylum' as 'spa' — Selina's preferred euphemism for an embarrassing situation
jokes.explanation: Gary's protective spin-doctoring is perfectly in character — he'd do anything to preserve Selina's self-image. 'Spa' as a euphemism for asylum is a sharp single-word joke with maximum economy. Four words, big laugh.

jokes.text: Wait. What? No. This is where you stick an unfuckable intern. / Oh, no, no, no. When I was a summer intern for Lieutenant Governor Plasko back in seventh grade, I was in a much bigger office.
jokes.setup: Dan shows Richard his new Lieutenant Governor office, which is tiny
jokes.punchline: Richard's complaint that he had a bigger office as a seventh-grade intern is both a character beat (Richard's strange political precocity) and an absurd detail
jokes.explanation: Two jokes stacked: 'unfuckable intern' as the metric for office quality, and Richard as a seventh-grade political intern. The latter adds to Richard's established character as someone who has been involved in local politics his entire life in the most unassuming way.

jokes.text: We gotta get the morbidly obese fuck out of Iowa. / Last night I tried to find one non-chain restaurant to eat at, and Yelp basically told me to go Fuddrucker myself.
jokes.setup: Dan complains about being stuck in Iowa
jokes.punchline: 'Yelp basically told me to go Fuddrucker myself' — using the chain restaurant name as a profanity substitute — is the comedic payoff
jokes.explanation: 'Go Fuddrucker yourself' is an inventive profanity substitute using the chain restaurant name — the kind of wordplay that makes you wish it was a real expression. Works well as both a regional dig and a verbal gag.

jokes.text: You think you're hot shit, Splett? First human mayor of Lurlene, straight to lieutenant governor in just a few months. / Thank you. / Well, now you're an ice cold dog turd, because you're not gettin' my job.
jokes.setup: The Iowa governor challenges Richard's rise
jokes.punchline: The 'hot shit / ice cold dog turd' binary is a vivid and original insult structure; Richard's earnest 'Thank you' in the middle of the threat adds a perfect beat
jokes.explanation: Richard's sincere 'Thank you' after being called 'hot shit' is perfectly in character — he takes it as a compliment before the insult arrives. 'Ice cold dog turd' is a vivid, specific escalation from the setup.

jokes.text: Save it, Manhattan Date Rape Mystery.
jokes.setup: Dan tries to intervene diplomatically with the Governor
jokes.punchline: Ballentine dismisses Dan with 'Manhattan Date Rape Mystery' — a characterization of Dan that implies exactly what it implies
jokes.explanation: 'Manhattan Date Rape Mystery' as a character epithet for Dan Egan is both devastating and accurate to his established character. The specificity — not just 'rapey' but 'Mystery,' implying unresolved allegations — makes it more unsettling and funnier. Extremely quotable. Economy is near-perfect: three words, complete character assassination.

jokes.text: Splett, I'm gonna bury you so deep in bullshit make-work people are gonna forget Iowa even has a lieutenant governor. / That shouldn't be too hard.
jokes.setup: The Governor threatens Richard with useless busy work
jokes.punchline: Richard's cheerful 'That shouldn't be too hard' agrees with the premise that the lieutenant governorship is inherently irrelevant
jokes.explanation: Richard's oblivious affirmation of his own irrelevance is perfectly in character. The joke works because Richard is agreeing with an insult as if it's simply a fact about his job. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: What's he doing? How long does it take to change an adult diaper?
jokes.setup: Someone is waiting for Mike to appear on his new TV show
jokes.punchline: The implication that Mike either is wearing adult diapers or is so old/incompetent that the question is reasonable
jokes.explanation: A fairly cheap age/incontinence joke about Mike. Gets a mild laugh but doesn't rise above the generic. Below the episode's typical level of craft.

jokes.text: Although publicly, Montez claims to be considering all options. Privately, I heard she's been doing impressions of you getting broom-sticked in prison.
jokes.setup: The White House refuses to help Selina avoid ICC prosecution
jokes.punchline: President Montez is privately mimicking Selina being sexually assaulted in prison — a savage detail about their rivalry
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'impressions' and 'broom-sticked' is sharp — it's not just that Montez wants Selina imprisoned, she's gleefully performing it for an audience. Dark and effective political rivalry comedy.

jokes.text: Are people actually laughing at that impression? / - No. - Yes.
jokes.setup: Selina hears Montez is doing impressions of her being assaulted
jokes.punchline: Ben and Kent give contradictory answers simultaneously — Kent's 'Yes' is the brutal punchline
jokes.explanation: The simultaneous contradictory answers (Ben protecting Selina's feelings vs. Kent's brutal honesty) is perfectly in character for both and delivers maximum impact in minimum words. Kent's 'Yes' is the funnier because his deadpan makes it more credible.

jokes.text: Just the prison of unmet potential.
jokes.setup: Selina tells Minna that you don't go to prison for not being head of the IMF
jokes.punchline: Minna's rejoinder — 'just the prison of unmet potential' — is a deadpan philosophical comeback that also happens to be poignant
jokes.explanation: A beautifully constructed line — Minna's philosophical precision applied to her personal disappointment. 'The prison of unmet potential' is the kind of line that works as both a joke and a genuine observation. Economy is near-perfect.

jokes.text: Norway. Ambassador Costas was Montez's husband's chiropractor. / Yeah, he does most of his ambassador-ing in Orlando.
jokes.setup: Selina needs to reach the American Ambassador to Norway
jokes.punchline: The ambassador is a political crony appointment — a chiropractor who works from Orlando
jokes.explanation: 'Does most of his ambassador-ing in Orlando' is a great line — the gerund 'ambassador-ing' plus the Orlando detail perfectly captures the absurdity of political crony appointments. Solid Veep political satire.

jokes.text: From the bottom of my heart, I truly and completely forgive you. / Now shut the living fuck up.
jokes.setup: Minna asks Selina a 'teeny tiny' question, and Selina prepares what seems like a heartfelt response
jokes.punchline: The elaborate build to 'I truly and completely forgive you' is immediately followed by 'Now shut the living fuck up' — the forgiveness is purely strategic
jokes.explanation: The structural beat here is excellent — the long pause before the prepared statement, then the immediate reversal. The fact that Selina's forgiveness is entirely transactional (she wants Minna to be quiet, not absolved) is perfectly revealed by the juxtaposition. The coexistence of sanctimony and profanity is classic Selina.

jokes.text: Amy: Streptococksucker.
jokes.setup: Amy apparently has an illness, revealed as chicken pox
jokes.punchline: Amy invents a venereal-disease-inflected name for her condition — 'Streptococksucker' — as her opening word in the scene
jokes.explanation: 'Streptococksucker' as a compound portmanteau blending strep throat and an expletive is genuinely inventive — it's a word that sounds medically plausible while being maximally vulgar. One word, perfectly delivered as an opener. Amy's profanity-as-coping is perfectly in character. Very quotable.

jokes.text: Oh, my God, you Patient IQ Zero! / You infected all those nutballs who don't believe in vaccinations! / Well, serves 'em right.
jokes.setup: A chicken pox outbreak has been traced to Harrisburg, PA — Jonah's rally location
jokes.punchline: The anti-vaccination candidate gave chickenpox to his own anti-vax supporters; Jonah's response is 'serves 'em right' — not even registering his own role
jokes.explanation: 'Patient IQ Zero' is a perfect neologism — combining 'Patient Zero' with an IQ insult. But the real joke is Jonah's complete cognitive dissociation: he infected people who don't believe in vaccines and his response is that THEY deserve it. The irony is airtight and perfectly executed.

jokes.text: Didn't you have chicken pox as a child?! / Were you too busy bed-wetting and cutting fuck-holes in watermelons?
jokes.setup: Amy demands to know if Jonah had chicken pox as a child
jokes.punchline: She suggests Jonah was too busy with infantile perversions to contract childhood illnesses normally
jokes.explanation: 'Cutting fuck-holes in watermelons' is an original and grotesque image — Amy's insults about Jonah's childhood are always more vivid than necessary. The specificity (watermelons, not just fruit) and the escalation from 'bed-wetting' to this is well-constructed.

jokes.text: Amy, that only works with fleshy melons. / Beth: Duh. / And sometimes pumpkins.
jokes.setup: Amy accuses Jonah of cutting fuck-holes in watermelons
jokes.punchline: Jonah doesn't deny it — he offers a helpful correction about which produce works best, and Beth confirms 'Duh' while adding pumpkins
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best exchanges. Amy's insult is meant to be an absurd hypothetical, but Jonah and Beth treat it as established fact and offer horticultural corrections. 'And sometimes pumpkins' from Beth is a perfect extension — she adds information as if contributing to a gardening discussion. The escalation (watermelons → fleshy melons → pumpkins) is impeccable, and Beth's 'Duh' is devastating in its casualness. Near-perfect construction.

jokes.text: Hi, Bethy, I'm really sorry I just showed up. / Oh, no. No! No, no, no! / I really wanna have a chance to talk with you, Jonah. / I hate you so much I could walk into a supermarket and shoot everybody.
jokes.setup: Jonah's biological father shows up unexpectedly
jokes.punchline: Jonah's expression of hatred is framed as a mass shooting threat — an absurdist expression of emotion that reveals Jonah's inability to express normal feelings without going to extremes
jokes.explanation: Jonah's violent threat as emotional expression is darkly funny and specific to his character. The mass shooting reference is edgy but contextually earned — it's clearly hyperbole from a character who expresses all emotions through aggression.

jokes.text: OK. / I b-b-beg your pardon? / OK, you can be my dad. / I can be what? / Aw, come here! Aw, I love you, Jonah. / I love you too, Daddy. No homo. / Sure. No homo.
jokes.setup: Lloyd delivers a sincere speech about wanting another chance; after a long pause Jonah replies
jokes.punchline: The comedic reversal — after threatening mass violence, Jonah instantly accepts his father — followed by 'No homo' applied to a father-son 'I love you' is both absurd and character-perfect. The father gamely echoes 'Sure. No homo.'
jokes.explanation: The comedic pivot from furious rejection to instant acceptance plays as a misdirection beat. 'No homo' applied to familial love is both a character note (Jonah's emotional illiteracy) and a good structural tag. The father's weary 'Sure. No homo' is the perfect button — he'll take what he can get.

jokes.text: Amerikkalainen war criminal syo kaikki. / Alleged war criminal.
jokes.setup: Selina's eaten someone's yogurt at the embassy
jokes.punchline: An embassy staffer writes 'American war criminal ate everything' in Finnish on the fridge; Selina's only objection is the word 'alleged'
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character comedy. Selina's only concern about being labeled a war criminal is the lack of legal precision — she doesn't dispute the substance, just the qualifier. 'Alleged war criminal' as her only objection to the fridge note encapsulates her character in one line. The Finnish language adds an extra layer of specificity.

jokes.text: Number one, you write your name on your food in the refrigerator. / You see here, I've written 'Minna,' and I have written it with a permanent marker. / Number two, if you use a bowl or a spoon or a plate or a cup or a jug, afterwards, you would wash in the sink.
jokes.setup: Minna is laying down embassy house rules for Selina
jokes.punchline: The absurdity of a former U.S. president being lectured on refrigerator labeling and dish-washing by the Finnish ambassador — the gap between Selina's self-image and her current status is the joke
jokes.explanation: Minna's exhaustive list of kitchen utensils ('bowl or a spoon or a plate or a cup or a jug') is both in character for her precision and funny for its mundanity applied to Selina's situation. The permanent marker detail is a perfect Minna touch.

jokes.text: Selina, if you go to prison, you will not have your Gary to clean up after you. / OK, look-- / No, I'll be there.
jokes.setup: Minna warns Selina she won't have Gary in prison
jokes.punchline: Gary immediately offers to be with Selina in prison — a devotion that is both touching and deeply pathetic
jokes.explanation: Gary's instant volunteering for prison to stay near Selina is his character distilled — it's simultaneously sweet and pathological. The interruption of Selina's protest ('OK, look--') by Gary's offer is well-timed.

jokes.text: I feel like the Grim Reaper just dropped his scythe and started eating me out!
jokes.setup: Selina learns her polling numbers have surged because Americans support her drone strike
jokes.punchline: She expresses her relief via a graphic sexual metaphor involving death personified
jokes.explanation: Exceptional. The Grim Reaper image is unexpected and absurdly specific — death dropping his scythe to pleasure her is a genuinely original metaphor for unexpected salvation. It's also deeply revealing about Selina's relationship with risk and power. The image is vivid, the economy is perfect, and only Selina would frame political relief in these terms. One of the episode's best lines.

jokes.text: It turns out Americans don't give a flying fatwa if you drone a bunch of Muslims! Your favorables have never been higher, ma'am. For polling purposes, you're practically a generic white male.
jokes.setup: Selina fears the war crimes accusations will destroy her politically
jokes.punchline: Ben reveals that Americans actually support drone strikes on Muslims, and the ultimate compliment for polling is 'generic white male'
jokes.explanation: 'Flying fatwa' as an alliterative expletive substitute is inventive and contextually perfect. 'Practically a generic white male' as a polling compliment is sharp satire about American political demographics. The line works on multiple satirical levels simultaneously.

jokes.text: Ma'am, we got bigger reindeer to fuck here.
jokes.setup: Selina gets excited about Catherine's wedding idea
jokes.punchline: 'Bigger reindeer to fuck' as a Norway-localized version of 'bigger fish to fry' — context-appropriate profanity upgrade to an idiom
jokes.explanation: Veep's signature mangled idiom, here upgraded for the Norwegian setting. 'Reindeer' is the right Norway-specific animal. Funny but not exceptional.

jokes.text: A wedding got me into this hot mess... a wedding's gonna get me out of it. / A Potemkin wedding, if I may coin a phrase. / Please don't.
jokes.setup: Selina hatches a plan to use Catherine's wedding as cover for her escape
jokes.punchline: 'Potemkin wedding' is a sharp coinage; the staffer's 'Please don't' — meaning 'please don't coin that phrase, it's terrible' — is the button
jokes.explanation: 'Potemkin wedding' is a clever historical reference applied to the scheme, and the staffer's preemptive rejection of the coinage is a good meta-comedy beat. Works well within the scene.

jokes.text: Yes. Well, this is the compound belonging to Hamza Al Bashir. Now, hold on. What is that right on over there, ma'am? Is that an elephant or something? / Yes. Yeah, I believe it is. Apparently, Al Bashir had some sort of a private zoo, and that's actually how we were able to locate him. / Mike: And boom! Wow! There goes the elephant.
jokes.setup: Selina is being interviewed on CBS News about the drone footage
jokes.punchline: The drone strike that killed 'wedding civilians' also destroyed a terrorist's private zoo — the elephant being hit on live television is played as entertainment by both Mike and Selina
jokes.explanation: The elephant in the drone footage is a perfect comic escalation — war crimes are one thing, but killing an elephant on camera is what gets Americans' attention (as the episode later explicitly acknowledges). Mike's 'Wow! There goes the elephant!' as sports commentary on a war crime is exactly right.

jokes.text: What do you think? Beige or Tuscan beige? / Tough call. / They are... the same.
jokes.setup: Catherine is choosing between fabric swatches
jokes.punchline: Catherine presents two identical colors as distinct options; after a 'tough call' pause, someone observes they are the same
jokes.explanation: A fairly standard 'colors are the same' joke — competent but unremarkable. The pause before the observation is the best part. Gets a mild chuckle.

jokes.text: Do you remember my 13th birthday? / No, I certainly don't. / Do you remember my 16th birthday, then? Do you remember that? / No, darling. Are we gonna go through all the birthdays? I don't remember any of them.
jokes.setup: Catherine is confronting Selina about years of maternal neglect
jokes.punchline: Selina doesn't pretend to remember selectively — she admits she remembers none of Catherine's birthdays, then gets slightly annoyed that Catherine is listing them all
jokes.explanation: 'Are we gonna go through all the birthdays? I don't remember any of them' is a brilliantly awful mother line. Selina doesn't pretend — she's not embarrassed, she's mildly inconvenienced by the exercise of listing birthdays she doesn't remember. The progressive 13th, 16th setup implies an exhaustive list is coming, and Selina short-circuits it. Perfect character integration.

jokes.text: I didn't know the theme of the wedding was Edward Scissor Sisters.
jokes.setup: Selina is insulting Catherine's wedding outfit
jokes.punchline: 'Edward Scissor Sisters' merges 'Edward Scissorhands' (the Tim Burton character) with 'Scissor Sisters' (the LGBT-adjacent pop act) — an insult that mocks both the aesthetic and the lesbian wedding simultaneously
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's most inventive wordplay jokes. 'Edward Scissor Sisters' is a perfect portmanteau insult that layers multiple references: the gothic aesthetics of Edward Scissorhands and the queer-coded Scissor Sisters band. It works as both a fashion critique and a homophobic jab, which tells us everything about Selina's complicated relationship with her daughter's sexuality. Near-perfect economy, highly quotable.

jokes.text: Those sandwiches aren't gonna finger themselves.
jokes.setup: Gary excuses himself from the awkward mother-daughter confrontation
jokes.punchline: His excuse to leave is that he needs to finger sandwiches — phrased as if 'finger' is the verb, creating a double entendre that Gary may not even intend
jokes.explanation: Gary's oblivious exit line is extremely well-constructed — it works whether he knows it's a double entendre or not (probably not). The image of Gary somberly tending to finger sandwiches while escaping maternal drama is perfect. Very quotable and pure Gary.

jokes.text: I am so very proud of you and your decision to get married... here.
jokes.setup: Selina is attempting a sincere moment with Catherine
jokes.punchline: The long pause before 'here' reveals Selina only supports the European wedding for her own legal/financial reasons (community property) — the word 'here' is the tell
jokes.explanation: A callback to the earlier community property joke — the pause before 'here' is visible and deliberate, confirming Selina's support is entirely self-interested. Works best on rewatch when you remember exactly why she cares about the European location. The economy of the pause-then-word is excellent.

jokes.text: And you look amazing. / Thank you. / And I'm amazed by your look.
jokes.setup: Selina is trying to be gracious to Catherine and Marjorie
jokes.punchline: Selina compliments Catherine genuinely, then delivers a completely different and vaguely threatening 'compliment' to Marjorie: 'I'm amazed by your look'
jokes.explanation: 'I'm amazed by your look' is a near-perfect passive-aggressive non-compliment — it sounds like a compliment but 'amazed' carries the freight of shock or bewilderment. The contrast with the genuine 'you look amazing' to Catherine is the structural key. Excellent economy: same words, completely different meaning.

jokes.text: Where's your mother? / She went to the bathroom. / That's not the bathroom.
jokes.setup: Selina appeared to go to freshen up
jokes.punchline: She has not gone to the bathroom — she's clearly escaped or done something else entirely. The revelation is played through the visual gag of her heading somewhere that isn't the bathroom.
jokes.explanation: A quick functional misdirection beat — we understand Selina is up to something without being told directly. Works as a scene transition more than a standalone joke.

jokes.text: Jonah Ryan has as much chance of becoming president as a stack of retarded raccoons in a trench coat, but if anyone's crawling out of our cesspool of a state to become his Ag Sec, it's gonna be this pigfucker.
jokes.setup: Ballentine is explaining why he's accompanying Dan to comfort Jonah
jokes.punchline: 'A stack of retarded raccoons in a trench coat' is an elaborate absurdist image for incompetence; 'pigfucker' applied to Richard as a compliment caps the joke
jokes.explanation: 'Stack of retarded raccoons in a trench coat' is the kind of specific absurdist insult that Veep excels at — it has an internal visual logic (raccoons stacked up wearing human clothing) that makes it both vivid and surreal. 'Pigfucker' as a term of grudging respect for Richard is also a good character note for Ballentine.

jokes.text: I didn't realize there was a job requirement.
jokes.setup: Ballentine calls Richard a 'pigfucker' as evidence he's qualified for Agriculture Secretary
jokes.punchline: Richard's deadpan response implies he's not sure if pigfucking is literally required, and he's not defensive about it either way
jokes.explanation: Richard's one-liner is perfect — the ambiguity between 'I wasn't aware literal pig intercourse was required' and 'I wasn't sure if there were qualifications for this position' creates a beautiful double reading. His deadpan delivery means he genuinely might mean either. Near-perfect economy.

jokes.text: What has your mother done now? / She used our wedding as a diversion to escape from the Norwegian National Police! / Smart.
jokes.setup: Catherine discovers Selina used the wedding as a cover for her escape
jokes.punchline: Marjorie's response to learning Selina used their wedding to evade international law enforcement is a single word: 'Smart.'
jokes.explanation: Marjorie's single-word response is perfect — she's not upset, she genuinely admires the tactical move. Her robotic, affectless character means 'Smart.' lands as both an admiring compliment and an alien response to the situation. Maximum economy, perfect character integration.

jokes.text: I'm sorry. Who taught you to do a smoky eye? / Joint Special Operations Command. JSOC. / Aha. It's not bad.
jokes.setup: Gary notices Marjorie's makeup for the wedding
jokes.punchline: Marjorie learned smoky eye makeup from special operations military training — she treats this as completely normal information to share about cosmetics
jokes.explanation: Exceptional character comedy. Marjorie's military background applied to beauty tips is perfectly in character — she approaches everything with the same clinical precision. 'Joint Special Operations Command. JSOC.' delivered as a makeup attribution is one of the episode's funniest moments. Gary's professional 'It's not bad' as the button is perfect. Near-perfect character integration and economy.

jokes.text: These tunnels date back to World War II. / Really? / Local citizens banded together in secret to assist the Nazis.
jokes.setup: The group is escaping through tunnels under Oslo
jokes.punchline: Murman explains the tunnels were used to help the Nazis — a reversal of the expected 'resistance tunnels' narrative, delivered with the same tour-guide cheerfulness
jokes.explanation: The reversal of the expected World War II tunnel narrative (resistance → collaboration) delivered as casual historical fact by Murman is darkly funny. His cheerful factual delivery of Nazi assistance is perfectly in character for the Georgian oligarch-turned-host.

jokes.text: Once, I told my enemy it was tunnel escape, but in fact, I just buried him alive. / Murman. / Actually, it was her, but story is funnier with a man, huh? / In these MeToo days, you know? / Actually, I think it could work with a woman. / Either way, it's a very good story. Beginning, middle and end. Plus a lesson.
jokes.setup: Murman is telling a reassuring story about tunnel escapes
jokes.punchline: He buried someone alive in a tunnel; then the group has an extended discussion about whether the gender of the victim affects the story's quality, including a reference to MeToo and a sincere story structure analysis ('Beginning, middle and end. Plus a lesson.')
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best extended exchanges. The joke has multiple layers: (1) the atrocity of burying someone alive as a casual anecdote, (2) the meta discussion about whether changing the victim's gender is a 'MeToo' consideration, (3) Murman's earnest literary analysis of his own story ('Beginning, middle and end. Plus a lesson.'). The 'Plus a lesson' is the perfect button. The MeToo awareness applied to a murder story is a brilliant satirical move. Near-perfect construction.

jokes.text: and the airplane is waiting to run out of gas and drop you into the North Sea. / What? / Just kidding.
jokes.setup: Murman is arranging their escape plane
jokes.punchline: He describes the plane crashing into the North Sea, pauses for reaction, then reveals it's a joke
jokes.explanation: A functional Murman character beat — his dark humor is consistent with the buried-alive story. The 'Just kidding' deflation works but isn't especially inventive.

jokes.text: Ma'am, that's the Nobel Prize of bad ideas! / No, that's economics, OK?
jokes.setup: Selina wants to stop and pick up her award while fleeing ICC jurisdiction
jokes.punchline: A staffer calls it 'the Nobel Prize of bad ideas'; Selina corrects the joke by pointing out there's actually a Nobel Prize for Economics — 'that's economics'
jokes.explanation: A tight two-part joke: the 'Nobel Prize of bad ideas' is good, but Selina's deflation ('No, that's economics') is the better half — it's technically accurate (the Nobel Economics prize is considered less prestigious) and reveals Selina knows more about Nobel prizes than she admitted earlier. The callback to her earlier dismissal of the prize distinction adds depth.

jokes.text: Oh, please, I've got America on my side, and America doesn't give a shit about anything!
jokes.setup: Her team warns she's still under ICC jurisdiction
jokes.punchline: Selina's 'America doesn't give a shit about anything' as a source of comfort and confidence is both a political observation and a joke about American exceptionalism
jokes.explanation: A sharp line about American isolationism and indifference to international law — Selina's nationalism is her actual legal defense strategy. The absolute conviction in her delivery is the comedy.

jokes.text: Kent: The Hague gives a shit. / Ben: We are not in America. We just crawled out of a drain!
jokes.setup: Selina insists America doesn't care about anything
jokes.punchline: Kent's flat 'The Hague gives a shit' is perfect deadpan; Ben's 'We just crawled out of a drain!' grounds the absurdity physically
jokes.explanation: Kent's four-word response is perfectly calibrated — he doesn't argue with her point, just identifies the entity that does give a shit. Ben's more physical reminder ('we just crawled out of a drain') adds a nice grounding detail.

jokes.text: Tell him to get his dick out of whatever homeless woman froze to death in front of the hotel, meet us over there with the speech.
jokes.setup: Selina needs to reach Leon for the acceptance speech
jokes.punchline: The extremely specific image of Leon with a frozen dead homeless woman is both a character assassination and an absurdly specific dark comedy image
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'froze to death in front of the hotel' is the joke — it's such a precise and terrible hypothetical that it works. Selina's casually contemptuous view of Leon's sex life is consistent with her treatment of all staff.

jokes.text: Hi, I'm Dan Egan, I used to work at the White House. / You barely worked at the White House.
jokes.setup: Dan introduces himself to someone
jokes.punchline: Amy undercuts his credential immediately — he 'barely' worked at the White House
jokes.explanation: A quick, competent exchange. Amy's dismissal of Dan's main credential is consistent with their dynamic, but the joke is relatively gentle compared to the episode's harder material.

jokes.text: Oh, Ames, I almost offered to help your career, then bang you and tell everybody in the office you're an unstable slut. / It's a new dress.
jokes.setup: Dan and Amy are having a tense reunion
jokes.punchline: Dan explicitly outlines his manipulative playbook (career help → sex → slut-shaming) as if it's a viable option he's generously declining; Amy's non-sequitur response ('It's a new dress') suggests she's completely over him
jokes.explanation: Dan explicitly narrating his sexual manipulation strategy as a compliment/threat is perfectly in character — his lack of self-awareness about how this sounds is the joke. Amy's 'It's a new dress' as a complete non-response is the perfect button: she's not even engaging with the threat, she's just noting her outfit. The juxtaposition is excellent.

jokes.text: How long have you been taking these? / Ever since Jonah whacked me on the nose at the rally, but the pain went away in two days. / They gave me 97 pills, though, so now I've just been taking 'em for the going-away of my feelings.
jokes.setup: Amy notices Beth is taking pills
jokes.punchline: 'The going-away of my feelings' as a phrase for emotional numbing via opioids is Beth's accidental poetic way of describing addiction
jokes.explanation: 'The going-away of my feelings' is a genuinely great phrase — it sounds like Beth's own construction, both grammatically awkward and oddly poignant. The detail that they gave her 97 pills sets up the punchline perfectly. Beth's cheerful admission of recreational opioid use is dark comedy done right.

jokes.text: So you're not pregnant. / Oh, no. Jonah and I don't want any more kids until I can get my cake pop business off the ground. And we can do genetic testing to make sure they're not born dead.
jokes.setup: Amy sees Beth taking pills and assumes she might be pregnant
jokes.punchline: 'Make sure they're not born dead' as a casualphrase for avoiding genetic disease is Beth's inadvertently grim take on family planning
jokes.explanation: The cake pop business caveat followed by 'not born dead' is a great double beat — Beth's priorities (cake pops first, viable offspring second) are a character portrait. 'Born dead' instead of 'stillborn' or 'with genetic conditions' is Beth's characteristically blunt way of expressing clinical concern.

jokes.text: Right now you have a historic opportunity to speak to our better natures, bring the country together. But to do that, I think you have to tone down the angry, incoherent rhetoric a bit. / I have kinda pushed it, haven't I? / A little bit, Jonie.
jokes.setup: Lloyd is trying to moderate Jonah's rhetoric
jokes.punchline: 'I have kinda pushed it, haven't I?' as Jonah's mild self-assessment of his campaign — which includes threatening mass shootings, spreading chicken pox, and promising to be the least-vaccinated state — is a masterpiece of understatement
jokes.explanation: Jonah's casual 'kinda pushed it' is a perfectly calibrated understatement after everything we've seen. Works as a character beat and a satirical observation about politicians' self-awareness.

jokes.text: Sorry you killed your own father and I wasn't there to see it!
jokes.setup: Uncle Jeff offers condolences at Lloyd's memorial
jokes.punchline: His 'condolences' are actually regret at missing Jonah accidentally infecting and killing his own father — delivered as a sincere expression of sympathy
jokes.explanation: Uncle Jeff's 'condolences' being entirely about his own disappointment at missing the spectacle of Jonah killing his father is a great character beat. The framing as regret rather than cruelty makes it funnier — he's not being mean, he's genuinely sad he missed it. Perfectly in character for the established Jeff.

jokes.text: You're a hard man to get ahold of. / Are we really doing this again? We all know he speaks English.
jokes.setup: Selina approaching Lu through a translator again
jokes.punchline: The translator pretense acknowledged as a pretense in a meta aside
jokes.explanation: A good satirical aside about diplomatic ritual. The meta-acknowledgment of the translator game is efficient.

jokes.text: If I'm elected, the U.S. is gonna import a shit-ton more of your tainted baby formula and defective drywall.
jokes.setup: Selina is trying to make a deal with President Lu for his political support
jokes.punchline: Selina describes American imports of Chinese goods in the most honest possible terms — 'tainted baby formula and defective drywall' — as diplomatic incentives
jokes.explanation: Selina's accidentally honest diplomatic pitch referencing real Chinese product safety scandals ('tainted baby formula' = 2008 melamine scandal) as positive trade incentives is sharp satire. The casual 'shit-ton' quantifier against specific product failures is well executed.

jokes.text: Different label, same product. / Islands. Any islands you want, Lu. Diaoyu, Spratly-- Florida Keys, even.
jokes.setup: Lu has just confirmed the tainted product strategy
jokes.punchline: Selina's escalating offer of territorial islands ends with 'Florida Keys, even' — offering American sovereign territory as casually as disputed South China Sea islands
jokes.explanation: The escalation from contested foreign islands to actual American territory is brilliantly constructed. 'Florida Keys, even' delivered as a bonus incentive treats American sovereignty as a bargaining chip equivalent to disputed sea features. Excellent political satire and strong escalation structure.

jokes.text: You can have Tibet. / What? / Yeah. You can have Tibet back. Are you fucking kidding me? / No. Right after the election, you can have it back.
jokes.setup: Selina is trying to convince Lu to support her over Montez; Lu says his loyalty to Montez is unshakeable
jokes.punchline: Selina offers to give Tibet back to China — the thing she freed, her entire legacy, her fake Nobel Prize rationale — as a political bargaining chip
jokes.explanation: The single best joke in the episode. The entire first act — the Nobel prize, the Tibetan lamas in the audience, the speeches about freeing Tibet — is setup for this moment. Selina casually offering to return Tibet to China, then clarifying 'I'll have to condemn your actions publicly, of course,' then '...but then we'll propose a U.N. resolution, you'll veto it... ya know, have at it' is the most complete expression of the show's thesis about Selina's character. It's a perfect callback that destroys everything she's built. Lu's quiet 'Are you fucking kidding me?' before accepting is also perfectly calibrated. The speech she gives while Tibetan lamas watch (later in the episode) becomes a darkly ironic callback to this moment. This is the episode's crown jewel.

jokes.text: I'll have to condemn your actions publicly, of course. / Of course. / But then we'll propose a U.N. resolution, you'll veto it... / I will. / then, ya know, have at it. / Is being president in a democracy even that great? / Well, it's barely a democracy, so... / I agree with that.
jokes.setup: Selina and Lu are negotiating the Tibet handover
jokes.punchline: 'Is being president in a democracy even that great? / Well, it's barely a democracy' is a casual philosophical exchange between two authoritarian-adjacent leaders about the value of democratic government
jokes.explanation: 'It's barely a democracy, so...' as Selina's pitch for why being president is worth wanting is both a great line and a devastating piece of political satire. Two leaders discussing whether American democracy even counts as democracy, in the context of a corrupt deal to return a nation to authoritarian control, while attending a Nobel Peace Prize ceremony. The layers are exceptional. 'I agree with that' from Lu is the perfect deadpan button.

jokes.text: 'Cause you're my lapdog now. / OK. / Yeah.
jokes.setup: Selina has just successfully negotiated the Tibet deal with Lu
jokes.punchline: After the diplomatic exchange, Selina drops the formality: 'you're my lapdog now.' Lu's mild 'OK. / Yeah.' acceptance is the joke — he's not even offended
jokes.explanation: Selina's triumphant 'you're my lapdog now' to the President of China — and his casual acceptance — is a great power dynamic joke. The informality after the elaborate diplomatic dance is the structural payoff.

jokes.text: Oh, God Almighty. I-- / So maybe I should just wrap this up. / Shall we scroll, um, ahead, please? / Could you just-- Yes. / Wait-- Yes. / Tibet must always be free. Free today, free tomorrow, free forever. Oh, God.
jokes.setup: Selina is giving her Nobel acceptance speech about Tibet, having just agreed to give Tibet back to China
jokes.punchline: The speech is going catastrophically wrong as she describes torture and imprisonment of Tibetans she's essentially just agreed to allow — the comedy of her trying to get through it is escalating cringe
jokes.explanation: The sustained cringe of Selina describing Tibetan torture to an audience that includes Tibetan lamas, while having just sold their freedom back to China, is the episode's sustained comic masterpiece. Each beat ('possibly even k-illed') where she hesitates, then skips forward in the speech, then the hollow 'Free today, free tomorrow, free forever. Oh, God.' is perfectly constructed escalating cringe. The callback potential to both the Tibet setup and the deal is exceptional.

jokes.text: No one's ever gonna forget that speech. / You're fired.
jokes.setup: Leon comments on the speech Selina just gave
jokes.punchline: Selina fires him immediately — his attempt at spin is the last straw
jokes.explanation: The ironic truth of 'no one's ever gonna forget that speech' immediately met with 'you're fired' is a good quick beat. Leon's spin instinct kicking in at the worst moment is consistent with his character.

jokes.text: Wow, talk about inhumane droning. / I guess I've got a type.
jokes.setup: Selina is reflecting on having just given a disastrous speech (the 'droning' of the speech echoing the literal drone strike)
jokes.punchline: 'I've got a type' — she's drawn to drone-related disasters, both literal and metaphorical
jokes.explanation: A self-aware callback that connects the drone strike to her 'droning' speech — 'I've got a type' as a dating/personality trope applied to disaster modes. The pun works on both levels and the self-awareness (rare for Selina) makes it surprising.

jokes.text: So, how was your follow-up appointment? / Less of a metal tube up my vag than this.
jokes.setup: Amy is forced to make small talk with her OB-GYN who is now dating Dan
jokes.punchline: Amy compares the gynecological appointment favorably to the current social situation — the metal speculum was less invasive than this awkward encounter
jokes.explanation: An outstanding line. Amy ranking a gynecological procedure above her current social discomfort is both graphically honest and structurally perfect. 'Less of a metal tube up my vag than this' as a comparative quality-of-life assessment is highly quotable and captures Amy's particular brand of bleak pragmatism.

jokes.text: Can you get her a pill or something and calm her down? / I don't want diazepam, I want something good.
jokes.setup: Nancy is grieving loudly at Lloyd's hospital bedside
jokes.punchline: Nancy refuses the sedative for not being strong enough — she knows her drugs
jokes.explanation: Nancy's pharmaceutical discernment in the midst of grief is a good dark joke. The specificity of rejecting diazepam by name is the key detail.

jokes.text: I always had you pegged as an AIDS guy.
jokes.setup: Jeff is laughing about Jonah killing Lloyd with chicken pox
jokes.punchline: Jeff's alternate theory about how Jonah would eventually kill someone is AIDS — suggesting he expected Jonah to be HIV-positive and infect partners
jokes.explanation: Jeff continues his extraordinary streak of inappropriate condolences. The logic that Jonah would eventually kill someone via infectious disease, but Jeff expected it to be AIDS, is both an insult and a darkly coherent prediction. Slightly cheaper than the previous Jeff line.

jokes.text: It's not funny, but his laughter is infectious.
jokes.setup: Jeff is laughing hysterically at the memorial
jokes.punchline: 'His laughter is infectious' at a memorial where the death was caused by an infectious disease (chicken pox) is a dark wordplay joke
jokes.explanation: A sharp contextual pun — 'infectious' meaning both 'contagious laughter' and 'literally transmitted a disease that killed someone' in the same scene. Clean economy, good structure.

jokes.text: Here's my hotel key. If you got a dick, I'll pay double.
jokes.setup: An attendant asks Jeff to come with them
jokes.punchline: Jeff's response to being escorted out is to offer to pay a hospital/security worker for sex — the 'if you got a dick' gender conditional adding to the characterization
jokes.explanation: Jeff's exit is appropriately deranged — propositioning the person removing him from the memorial is consistent with his character. 'If you got a dick, I'll pay double' implies a preference that adds an extra layer to his already transgressive character.

jokes.text: As most of you know, I didn't get a lot of time with my dad, but I think in the time that I did have with him I realized what a stupid loser he was. He didn't deserve me. Or even my mom. All he ever did, actually, was run out on us. First, all those years ago, and now by dying.
jokes.setup: Jonah is giving what should be a memorial eulogy
jokes.punchline: He insults the recently deceased ('stupid loser,' 'didn't deserve me'), accuses him of abandonment, and frames the death as yet another form of running out on the family
jokes.explanation: 'And now by dying' is the episode's best eulogy line — the escalation from 'running out on us years ago' to 'running out on us by dying' treats death as a personal slight against Jonah. The entire speech is a character masterpiece: Jonah cannot even fake conventional grief because he lacks the emotional vocabulary. 'He didn't deserve me' at a memorial is deeply specific to Jonah's narcissism.

jokes.text: And I guess I don't even know if I liked him that much, because he probably woulda sucked like all of my other step-dads. And I don't care that he's dead, because I am very strong.
jokes.setup: Jonah's eulogy continues
jokes.punchline: 'I am very strong' as the reason he doesn't care that his father is dead is a magnificent self-delusion — the speech is visibly emotional while claiming invulnerability
jokes.explanation: The continuation of the eulogy — 'I am very strong' as the reason for not caring — is perfect Jonah. It's the kind of fragile masculinity self-declaration that reads as sincere to Jonah and devastatingly funny to everyone else. The contrast between the speech's content and its emotional delivery (clearly upset) makes it cringe comedy at its finest.

jokes.text: We're having cake pops at the house later. / Beth: Who wants to go dancing? My husband's right there.
jokes.setup: Jonah concludes his disastrous eulogy
jokes.punchline: The memorial concludes with Jonah advertising cake pops and Beth inviting people dancing while gesturing to her alive husband — the tonal mismatch is the joke
jokes.explanation: The pivot from eulogy to 'cake pops' and 'dancing' is a solid tonal joke. Beth's 'My husband's right there' at a funeral where he's not (Lloyd just died) suggests she's confused about whose memorial this is, which is an extra layer.

jokes.text: Richard, that bastard Ballentine, he caught shingles from Typhoid Jonah. He went blind with partial paralysis and has to step down. / Oh, my God, that's awful. / No! That makes me-- I mean, you, the governor of Iowa!
jokes.setup: Dan has news about Governor Ballentine
jokes.punchline: Dan's 'No! That makes me-- I mean, you, the governor of Iowa!' reveals his self-interest before catching himself — the 'I mean, you' correction is the joke
jokes.explanation: Dan's slip — celebrating the governorship as if it's his own before correcting to 'you' — is a perfect character beat. The 'Typhoid Jonah' nickname is also a nice touch. Richard's 'Oh, my God, that's awful' genuine compassion versus Dan's naked opportunism is a good contrast.

jokes.text: There are two things Americans don't like to see get hurt: animals, and white girls on spring break.
jokes.setup: Selina thinks her drone story has blown over because Americans don't care about drone strikes on Muslims
jokes.punchline: Kent identifies the two protected categories in American media sympathy: animals and 'white girls on spring break' — and the elephant footage violates the first category
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's most quotable lines and sharpest satirical observations. Kent's clinical delivery of 'animals, and white girls on spring break' as the binary of American media sympathy is precise, accurate, and devastating. The parallel structure is perfect, and the line carries genuine satirical weight beyond the episode. Near-perfect across all metrics.

jokes.text: Peggy Noonan has a column about Babar and American exceptionalism. / She's a dumb cunt.
jokes.setup: Kent reports that the elephant story is dominating media coverage
jokes.punchline: Selina's dismissal of Peggy Noonan is both a political joke (Noonan is a noted conservative commentator) and a character moment — 'dumb cunt' as a snap judgment on a serious columnist writing about Babar
jokes.explanation: The 'Babar and American exceptionalism' column title is a perfect absurdist media detail — Peggy Noonan's grandiose rhetorical style applied to a cartoon elephant. Selina's snap 'dumb cunt' response is both character and a political joke. Works well.

jokes.text: Is this gonna affect my numbers, ya think? / Yes.
jokes.setup: Selina sees she's getting the finger from people in the crowd
jokes.punchline: Kent's single unqualified 'Yes' is delivered without elaboration or softening
jokes.explanation: Kent's total deadpan 'Yes' — no hedging, no diplomacy — is good character comedy. One of his funnier one-word responses in the episode.

jokes.text: He's giving me the finger. Look. See?
jokes.setup: Richard Splett appears on TV apparently giving the camera the finger (accidentally or in a photo)
jokes.punchline: Selina's indignant interpretation of what is likely an innocent gesture from Richard as a personal slight
jokes.explanation: A functional visual gag — Selina's paranoid reading of Richard's gesture. Mild but consistent with her character.

jokes.text: And when I say Lord, I mean Jesus Christ Almighty, not the Jewish one.
jokes.setup: Jonah gives a speech at the memorial invoking 'the Lord'
jokes.punchline: He clarifies he means the Christian Jesus, not the Jewish God — at a memorial with apparently Jewish attendees (previously established Orthodox Jews as his coalition)
jokes.explanation: The clarification — 'not the Jewish one' — at what may be a partially Jewish gathering is both deeply offensive and perfectly in character for Jonah. The grammatical construction (Jesus Christ Almighty vs. 'the Jewish one') treats the religions as product variants. Jonah's complete unawareness of the offense is essential.

jokes.text: And they do-- that's just science. / But the other real killer... is diseases. / And how do these diseases get into America? / All: How? / Immigrants. / Man: Kill 'em! / Yeah-- Well, I mean, we don't have to kill all of 'em. There are some good immigrants. Beyoncé? / Ms. Brookheimer, do you have anything to eat? / Um, I've got gum and Advil.
jokes.setup: Jonah is addressing the vaccine debate
jokes.punchline: Jonah pivots from vaccines to immigrants as disease vectors; a crowd member calls for killing immigrants; Jonah partially walks it back ('we don't have to kill all of 'em') while offering Beyoncé as a 'good immigrant'; Amy's mundane aside ('I've got gum and Advil') is a perfect deadpan reaction
jokes.explanation: A sustained sequence of escalating political satire. Each beat tops the last: vaccines kill → diseases are the real killer → immigrants bring diseases → kill them all → well not ALL of them → Beyoncé (who is American-born) as the one good immigrant example. Amy's gum-and-Advil offer to a staffer over the genocide discussion is the perfect comedy of indifference. The 'He had this carne asada that I think was a family recipe. That guy stays.' tag is also excellent. One of the episode's best sustained comedy sequences.

jokes.text: He had this carne asada that I think was a family recipe. That guy stays.
jokes.setup: Jonah has just said there are some good immigrants, and named Beyoncé
jokes.punchline: His second good immigrant exception is specifically an unnamed person who made him good carne asada — his immigration policy is literally based on personal food preferences
jokes.explanation: A perfect extension of the previous joke. Jonah's immigration exemptions have now reduced to: Beyoncé (wrong, she's American) and the specific guy who makes him good carne asada. 'That guy stays' as an immigration ruling is one of the episode's best lines — it encapsulates the arbitrary cruelty of nativist politics through pure selfishness. Extremely quotable, outstanding character integration.

jokes.text: From now on, no one in, no one out! / Thank you. No one in, no one out!
jokes.setup: Jonah's anti-immigration speech reaches its climax
jokes.punchline: 'No one in, no one out' as an immigration policy is accidentally a perfect description of a sealed authoritarian state
jokes.explanation: 'No one in, no one out' sounds like a strong policy but is functionally North Korea. The crowd cheers. Functional political satire but less sharp than the carne asada line that preceded it.

jokes.text: Word is out on you, Splett. You're living proof that the worst people in politics always succeed. / I'll tell ya something else, Splett. Listen carefully. / Someday you'll be an old man, and you'll--
jokes.setup: Ballentine visits the hospitalized Splett
jokes.punchline: Ballentine begins what sounds like a final wisdom speech — interrupted by Dan getting him off topic
jokes.explanation: 'The worst people in politics always succeed' as an observation from a man going blind from shingles has genuine satirical resonance — it's one of Veep's core themes. The interrupted deathbed speech format adds structure.

jokes.text: Pull up a chair, Splett. I've never told anyone this before. / I have a daughter in California. She doesn't know anything about me. / I've seen photographs... on the internet.
jokes.setup: Ballentine wants to tell Richard something in confidence
jokes.punchline: His deep secret — 'I have a daughter in California' — is anticlimactic, and 'I've seen photographs... on the internet' as his only connection to her is both sad and funny
jokes.explanation: The dramatic setup ('I've never told anyone this before') promises a major revelation; 'I have a daughter in California' is both anticlimactic and character-revealing. 'I've seen photographs... on the internet' is the funnier second beat — the pause before 'on the internet' suggests he's using social media to stalk his own daughter from a distance.

jokes.text: Did President Meyer find a way to steal the nomination from me like she stole South Carolina? / If you can't figure out how to steal South Carolina, you have no business being president.
jokes.setup: Montez confronting what Selina has done
jokes.punchline: Kent's response reduces electoral fraud to a competency test — if you can't steal South Carolina, you're not presidential material
jokes.explanation: Kent's deadpan reduction of election theft to a leadership quality test is excellent. The line perfectly captures the show's cynicism about American democracy — electoral fraud isn't wrong, it's incompetent if you get caught or fail at it.

jokes.text: That's not funny, Kent. / I haven't been funny since 1987.
jokes.setup: Selina accuses Kent of being funny about something
jokes.punchline: Kent's deadpan claim that he hasn't been funny since 1987 — delivered with total sincerity — is simultaneously self-aware and in character
jokes.explanation: A classic Kent line — deadpan self-assessment delivered with total conviction. 'Since 1987' has the perfect specificity; it implies there was a moment when Kent stopped being funny, and he knows exactly when it was. The character integration is near-perfect: this line only works coming from Kent's robotic affect. Very quotable.

jokes.text: I didn't come to North Carolina to lose. I don't even like to change planes here.
jokes.setup: Selina is at an undisclosed location, insisting she won't lose
jokes.punchline: Her declaration of determination is undermined by its follow-up: she doesn't even like changing planes in North Carolina — a petty personal grievance elevated to political principle
jokes.explanation: The juxtaposition of political determination with 'I don't even like to change planes here' is perfectly Selina — her sense of regional grievance extends even to airport layovers. The specificity of Charlotte/RDU as an airport she dislikes for personal reasons giving her political resolve is a sharp character note.

jokes.text: I just wanna be president. / Do you want six almonds? / No!
jokes.setup: Selina expresses her core desire — the presidency
jokes.punchline: Gary's response to Selina's most sincere statement of ambition is to offer six almonds — the bathos is the entire joke
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best closing jokes. Selina's moment of raw vulnerability ('I just wanna be president') is met with Gary's most Gary possible response: six almonds. The bathos is perfect — Gary's care for Selina is entirely expressed through snacks, completely missing the emotional register of the moment. The specificity of 'six almonds' (not 'a snack,' not 'some nuts') is the final touch. Near-perfect economy, structure, and character integration. Beautifully closes the episode.

tmdb_overview: Selina tries to arrange a meeting via her Finnish contact Minna. Gary helps Catherine plan a big event. Jonah and Amy push an anti-vax message in Pennsylvania. Mike finds a new home. Richard may have an enemy.
insight: Ninety jokes in forty-three minutes—Veep's densest finale run-up hits series peak velocity.
